ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tau sar piah. Mapped to the broader 'pastry food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A species-level term for Teochew bean paste pastry is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tau sar piah. A0FJP (Pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for tau sar piah or Teochew bean paste pastry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tau sar piah piece weighs approximately 50 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold by traditional Singaporean bakeries and confectionery shops.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Sar Piah (Salty Bean) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values are accepted as exact match from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo). Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 12 g/100 g based on the known fat profile of lard/shortening-based Teochew pastry dough; this specific sub-fraction is not separately reported in the HPB entry and is therefore marked as an adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: Biscuits, shortbread — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to tau sar piah flaky pastry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tau sar piah (salty bean) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food category: shortbread/flaky wheat-flour pastry with sweetened filling (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 49.81 × 0.50) / 100 ≈ 16.2 for a 50 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (19.75 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.55 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (~12 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness; sodium at 196 mg/100 g adds a direct pressor load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit tau sar piah due to its combination of high refined sugar, high saturated fat, and moderate sodium, all of which independently and synergistically elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High available carbohydrate (49.81 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.55 g/100 g) and high sugar (19.75 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load (GL ~16 per 50 g piece), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid tau sar piah or restrict intake to very small occasional portions, as its high refined carbohydrate, high sugar, and low fibre content will cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~12 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from shortening raise LDL cholesterol and promote endothelial dysfunction; high sugar further contributes to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid tau sar piah due to its high saturated fat, estimated trans fat from shortening, and high sugar content, which collectively worsen lipid profiles and increase cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), saturated fat (~12 g/100 g), and refined sugar (19.75 g/100 g) are all pro-inflammatory and may exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid tau sar piah, particularly versions made with lard or hydrogenated shortening, as the combined pro-inflammatory fat and sugar profile can worsen disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau sar piah (salty bean paste pastry), known in Chinese as 豆沙饼（咸）, is a traditional Teochew Chinese baked pastry widely sold in Singapore and Malaysia. It consists of a multi-layered, flaky wheat-flour pastry shell made with lard or vegetable shortening, encasing a savoury-sweet filling of salted mung bean (green bean) paste. The pastry is baked until golden and crisp, with a characteristic crumbly, melt-in-the-mouth texture. The salty variant is distinguished from the sweet (teem) version by its more savoury, lightly salted bean filling, though both contain significant sugar. Per 100 g, tau sar piah (salty) provides approximately 500 kcal, 29.7 g fat (of which ~12 g is saturated), 49.81 g carbohydrate, 19.75 g sugar, 8.25 g protein, 1.55 g dietary fibre, and 196 mg sodium. The high fat content derives from the layered shortening-based pastry dough; the high sugar and carbohydrate from the sweetened bean paste filling. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycaemic load (~16 per 50 g piece), high refined sugar, and low fibre. It is also a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat, estimated trans fat from shortening, and moderate sodium. Tau sar piah is a culturally significant Teochew confection, commonly gifted during festivals and available year-round at traditional Singaporean bakeries. It is generally considered vegetarian but not vegan (may contain lard) and is not halal-certified in traditional preparations.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is an adapted estimate based on known fat profile of lard/shortening-based Teochew pastry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using shortbread/flaky pastry as the closest approved comparator. All other fields are grounded in approved sources or documented as estimated.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid sub-fraction, and moisture data for tau sar piah (salty bean) are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) entry for this item as of 2026; these fields are confirmed unavailable in the HPB Foodo database for this food record. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 g/100 g as an adapted estimate based on the known trans fat contribution of hydrogenated vegetable shortening used in traditional Teochew pastry dough; this value is not directly reported in the HPB entry. saturated_fat_g set to 12.0 g/100 g as an adapted estimate from the known saturated fat profile of lard/shortening-based pastry dough; not separately reported in the HPB entry. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using shortbread/flaky wheat-flour pastry as the closest approved comparator; no direct GI measurement exists for tau sar piah in approved sources. is_halal set to false as traditional preparations commonly use lard; halal-certified versions exist commercially but cannot be assumed for the generic record. is_vegan set to false as lard is a common traditional ingredient. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to closest_parent as no exact ontology term exists for tau sar piah; gap notes provided for both.
